cited_work.title: iTunes and citation metadata.
cited_work.abstract: Stumbled across a really nice paper (Why can't I manage academic papers like MP3s?) while reading commentary on Tim O'Reilley's post about FreeBase. In response to Danny Ayer's post, Tim O'Reilly wrote Howison and Goodrum make some interesting points, especially about how easy it is to get (or create) metadata for a CD, especially when compared to handling academic literature.

claims.claim_text: Prior work reports that Description of online digital content is currently extremely necessary to facilitate a diverse amount of resource sharing over the internet.

cited_work.title: Virtual Communities as Contributors for Digital Objects Metadata Generation
cited_work.abstract: Description of online digital content is currently extremely necessary to facilitate a diverse amount of resource sharing over the internet. Many times, content is shared and reused within a virtual community. Virtual communities tend to have their own specific needs of resources, and tend to use a specific vocabulary to describe content. Members of virtual communities also tend to have specific motivations for participating and sharing information and knowledge with other members. In this chapter, the authors discuss the benefits of community members generating content description by analyzing the current literature on the matter. Then, the authors present two studies they have held where they assess the metadata generated by users of an IPTV system and by members of two different virtual communities.

claims.claim_text: Prior work reports that Abstract Large language models (LLMs) reproduce some bibliographic records verbatim while fabricating others, with reproduction probability scaling in pretraining frequency and citation count as the standard proxy.

cited_work.title: Cross-Model Memorization Thresholds in Citation Generation: Evidence from Field-Level Cloze on Bibliographic Records
cited_work.abstract: Abstract Large language models (LLMs) reproduce some bibliographic records verbatim while fabricating others, with reproduction probability scaling in pretraining frequency and citation count as the standard proxy. A prior study reports GPT-4.1's first-author memorization begins near 90 citations on a model-chosen test set, leaving generalizability open. We sample 1,118 computer science and 1,079 oncology papers from OpenAlex, stratified by citation count, and run a field-level cloze task on eight bibliographic fields with strict binary scoring. Seven conditions are evaluated: three frontier flash-class models (Gemini 3 Flash Preview, GPT-5.4-mini, DeepSeek V4-Flash), the full-tier GPT-5.4, and their reasoning-enabled variants. The field hierarchy replicates across conditions. The first-author inflection spans 87–502 citations in computer science across Gemini, DeepSeek, and full-tier GPT-5.4, with GPT-5.4-mini a further decade higher at ∼1,500, a one-to-two-order-of-magnitude range. The outlier is the mini tier, not the GPT family or open/closed status. Reasoning is neutral for Gemini, adverse for DeepSeek, but substantially helpful for GPT-5.4-mini, shifting its curve left by nearly a decade: reaching records direct decoding cannot, not adding knowledge. The same hierarchy, mini outlier, and order-of-magnitude spread recur on oncology at uniformly lower recovery. Memorization thresholds are model-, mode-, and domain-specific, not universal.

claims.claim_text: Prior work reports that AI holds promise for transforming scientific processes, including hypothesis generation.

cited_work.title: Literature Meets Data: A Synergistic Approach to Hypothesis Generation
cited_work.abstract: AI holds promise for transforming scientific processes, including hypothesis generation. Prior work on hypothesis generation can be broadly categorized into theory-driven and data-driven approaches. While both have proven effective in generating novel and plausible hypotheses, it remains an open question whether they can complement each other. To address this, we develop the first method that combines literature-based insights with data to perform LLM-powered hypothesis generation. We apply our method on five different datasets and demonstrate that integrating literature and data outperforms other baselines (8.97\% over few-shot, 15.75\% over literature-based alone, and 3.37\% over data-driven alone). Additionally, we conduct the first human evaluation to assess the utility of LLM-generated hypotheses in assisting human decision-making on two challenging tasks: deception detection and AI generated content detection. Our results show that human accuracy improves significantly by 7.44\% and 14.19\% on these tasks, respectively. These findings suggest that integrating literature-based and data-driven approaches provides a comprehensive and nuanced framework for hypothesis generation and could open new avenues for scientific inquiry.
